电动卡车制造商尼古拉(Nikola)在9月份遭到广泛嘲笑，因为一家卖空公司透露，其第一辆卡车尼古拉一号(Nikola One)从未奏效。一段卡车运动的宣传片实际上显示了这辆车从山上滚下来。
但该公司周一发布的最新季度财务报告显示，尼古拉拥有2016年没有的东西：一车现金。具体地说，尼古拉在银行有9亿美元-其中大部分是在该公司6月份上市时筹集的。
尼古拉表示，它在2020年第三季度花费了1.17亿美元，但没有带来任何收入。损失比一些分析师预期的要小，投资者对这一结果反应积极，导致尼古拉的股价在盘后交易中上涨了2%。
尼古拉的资金储备意味着，尽管该公司的起源一团糟，尼古拉仍有机会成为一家有生存能力的卡车制造商。按照目前的消耗速度，该公司的现金应该可以维持两年左右。因此，尼古拉必须在2022年之前开始创造收入，或者从投资者那里筹集更多资金。
在尼古拉创始人特雷弗·米尔顿(Trevor Milton)的领导下，尼古拉试图同时推出几款产品。但是，尽管按照大多数标准，9亿美元是一大笔钱，但它可能不足以将多款汽车推向市场。弥尔顿早在9月份就不光彩地辞职了，从那以后，该公司似乎缩小了关注范围。
尼古拉的战略一直是与更有经验的卡车制造商合作，以获得设计和制造卡车的帮助。在公布第三季度财务业绩时，尼古拉专注于与意大利卡车制造商依维柯(依维柯)合作，生产一款名为尼古拉Tre的电池电动半卡车。尼古拉表示，该公司最近完成了Tre原型的组装，并预计在今年年底前完成另外四款。
尼古拉预计明年将开始在尼古拉位于亚利桑那州的制造厂生产Tre，该工厂目前正在建设中。根据周一提交给美国证券交易委员会(Securities And Exchange Commission)的一份文件，该公司希望在2021年末开始发货尼古拉Tre。
尼古拉表示，它仍未与通用汽车(General Motors)达成协议，该协议原定于9月30日前完成。周一的新闻稿没有包括任何关于通用汽车为尼古拉制造的獾皮卡的其他讨论。这与尼古拉首席执行官马克·拉塞尔上个月发表的声明是一致的，他说獾是一个有趣而令人兴奋的项目，但尼古拉专注于重型卡车和氢气基础设施。
在这一点上，如果尼古拉只是让它与通用汽车的交易失效，我不会感到惊讶。这笔交易在9月份宣布后，尼古拉的股价飙升。但这将需要尼古拉向通用汽车支付数亿美元-尼古拉可能需要现金才能将Tre推向市场。
尼古拉的第三个主要产品应该是名为尼古拉2的氢燃料电池汽车。通用汽车本应向尼古拉供应氢燃料电池，因此如果交易失败，尼古拉就会有问题。但尼古拉二代要到2023年末才会上市，这让尼古拉有充足的时间开发必要的技术，或者从其他合作伙伴那里获得技术。
尼古拉的氢气卡车业务也有可能失败。据报道，尼古拉一直在为计划中的氢气加气站网络寻找合作伙伴。在特雷弗·米尔顿(Trevor Milton)的领导下，尼古拉声称已经找到了生产大幅便宜的氢燃料的方法，但有关细节尚未公布。如果氢气卡车业务被证明不可行，尼古拉或许可以求助于销售电池电动卡车。
对尼古拉来说，更大的问题是，从长远来看，付钱给其他公司制造卡车不太可能是一种非常有利可图的商业模式。目前还不清楚尼古拉是否已经开发了自己的重要技术，或者它是否会在未来几年内做到这一点。但有了9亿美元的银行资金和更现实的路线图，尼古拉至少有机会发展成为自2016年以来一直假装的有生存能力的电动卡车公司。

Electric truckmaker Nikola was widely ridiculed in September when a short-selling firm  revealed that its first truck, the Nikola One, never worked. A promotional video of the truck "in motion" actually showed the vehicle  rolling down a hill.
 But the company's latest quarterly financial results,  released Monday, show that Nikola has something it didn't have in 2016: truckloads of cash. Specifically, Nikola has $900 million in the bank—most of it raised when the company  went public back in June.
 Nikola says it spent $117 million in the third quarter of 2020 while bringing in no revenue. The losses were smaller than some analysts had expected, and investors reacted positively to the results, sending Nikola's stock price up by 2 percent in after-hours trading.
 Nikola's war chest means that, despite the company's shambolic origins, Nikola might still have a shot to become a viable truckmaker. The company's cash should last about two years at the current burn rate. So Nikola has until 2022 to either begin generating revenues or raise more money from investors.
   Under Nikola founder Trevor Milton, Nikola was trying to launch several products at the same time. But while $900 million is a lot of money by most standards, it's probably not enough money to bring multiple vehicles to market. Milton  resigned in disgrace back in September, and since then the company seems to have narrowed its focus.
 Nikola's strategy has been to partner with more experienced truckmakers to get help designing and building its trucks. In announcing its third quarter financial results, Nikola focused on its partnership with Italian truckmaker Iveco to build a battery electric semi truck called the NIkola Tre. Nikola says it recently completed assembly of the Tre prototype and expects to complete four more before the end of the year.
 Nikola expects to begin manufacturing the Tre at Nikola's manufacturing plant—currently under construction in Arizona—next year. The company hopes to begin shipping the Nikola Tre in "late 2021," according to a  Monday filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission.
 Nikola says it still  hasn't closed a deal with General Motors that was originally scheduled to close by September 30. Monday's press release doesn't include any other discussion of the Badger pickup truck GM was supposed to build for Nikola. That's consistent with  statements made last month by Nikola CEO Mark Russell that the Badger was an "interesting and exciting project" but that Nikola was "focused on heavy trucks and hydrogen infrastructure.”
 At this point I wouldn't be surprised if Nikola simply allowed its deal with GM to lapse. The deal sent Nikola's stock soaring when it was  announced in September. But it would require Nikola to pay GM hundreds of millions of dollars—cash Nikola likely needs to bring the Tre to market.
   Nikola's third major product is supposed to be a hydrogen fuel cell vehicle called the Nikola Two. GM was supposed to supply Nikola with hydrogen fuel cells, so Nikola would have a problem if the deal fell through. But the Nikola Two isn't due out until late 2023, giving Nikola plenty of time to develop the necessary technology—or acquire it from another partner.
 It's also possible that Nikola's hydrogen trucking business won't pan out at all. Nikola has reportedly  struggled to find a partner for a planned network of hydrogen fueling stations. Under Trevor Milton, Nikola claimed it had figured out how to produce dramatically cheaper hydrogen fuel, but details about this have not been forthcoming. If the hydrogen trucking business proves not to be viable Nikola might be able to fall back on selling battery electric trucks.
 The larger issue for Nikola is that paying other companies to build trucks isn't likely to be a very lucrative business model in the long run. It's not clear if Nikola has developed any significant technologies of its own, or if it will manage to do so in the next couple of years. But with $900 million in the bank and a more realistic roadmap, Nikola at least has a chance to develop into the viable electric truck company it has pretended to be since 2016.
title: 尼古拉在银行有9亿美元的存款，上个季度亏损了1.17亿美元
title_en: Nikola has $900 million in the bank, lost $117 million last quarter

Go中的接口允许我们暂时将不同类型视为相同的数据类型。它们是围棋程序员工具带的核心，经常被新的围棋开发人员…不当使用。导致难以阅读和错误代码。让我们来看看Golang接口的一些最佳实践。
我经常把标准库作为编写干净Go的一个例子。标准错误接口很简单：
Error接口封装任何定义了Error()方法的类型。该方法不接受任何参数，并返回一个字符串。例如，让我们定义一个表示网络问题的结构：
Func(NP NetworkProblem)error()string{return fmt.Sprintf(&#34；network error！消息：%s，代码：%v&#34；，np.message，np.code)}。
现在，我们可以在任何接受错误的地方使用networkProblem结构的实例。
Func handleErr(Err Error){fmt.Println(err.Error())}np：=networkProblem{Message：&#34；我们收到问题&#34；，代码：404，}handleErr(NP)//打印&#34；网络错误！消息：我们收到一个问题，代码：404&#34；
如果您从本文中只学到了一条建议，那就是：保持接口小！接口旨在定义准确表示思想或概念所需的最小行为。
以下是一个较大接口的标准HTTP包中的一个示例，它是定义最小行为的一个很好的示例：
任何满足接口行为的类型都可以被HTTP包视为文件。这很方便，因为HTTP包不需要知道它正在处理的是磁盘上的文件、网络缓冲区还是简单的[]字节。
接口应该定义其他类型需要什么才能分类为该接口的成员。他们不应该知道在设计时恰好满足接口的任何类型。
例如，假设我们正在构建一个接口来描述定义汽车所需的组件。
GetColor()和GetSpeed()非常有意义，它们都是被限制在汽车范围内的方法。IsFireTruck()是一种反模式。我们正强制所有车辆申报是否为消防车。为了让这个模式有任何意义，我们需要一个可能的子类型的完整列表。IsPickUp()、IsSedan()、IsTank()…。它在哪里结束？？
相反，当给定Car接口的实例时，开发人员应该依赖类型断言的本机功能来派生底层类型。或者，如果需要子接口，可以将其定义为：
它继承了Car所需的方法，并添加了一个额外的所需方法以使Car成为消防车。
接口没有需要创建或销毁数据的构造函数或解构函数。
接口本质上不是分层的，尽管创建恰好是其他接口超集的接口有语法上的好处。
接口定义函数签名，但不定义底层行为。创建接口通常不会使您的代码在结构方法方面枯竭。例如，如果五种类型满足Error接口，则它们都需要各自版本的error()函数。
note_en:
Interfaces in Go allow us to treat different types as the same data type temporarily. They are central to a Go programmers toolbelt and are often used improperly by new Go developers… leading to hard to read and buggy code. Let’s take a look at some of the best practices for Golang interfaces.
  I often look to the standard library as an example of the way to write clean Go. The standard error interface is simple:
  The error interface encapsulates any type that has an  Error() method defined on it. That method accepts no parameters, and returns a  string. For example, let’s define a struct that represents a network problem:
   func (np networkProblem) Error() string {	return fmt.Sprintf("network error! message: %s, code: %v", np.message, np.code)}
 Now, we can use an instance of the  networkProblem struct wherever an error is accepted.
 func handleErr(err error) {	fmt.Println(err.Error())}np := networkProblem{	message: "we received a problem",	code: 404,}handleErr(np)// prints "network error! message: we received a problem, code: 404"
  If there is only one piece of advice that you take away from this article, make it this:  keep interfaces small! Interfaces are meant to define the  minimal behavior necessary to accurately represent an idea or concept.
 Here is an example from the standard  HTTP package of a larger interface that’s a good example of defining minimal behavior:
  Any type that satisfies the interface’s behaviors can be considered by the HTTP package as a  File. This is convenient because the HTTP package doesn’t need to know if it’s dealing with a file on disk, a network buffer, or a simple  []byte.
  An interface should define what is necessary for other types to classify as a member of that interface. They shouldn’t be aware of any types that happen to satisfy the interface at design time.
 For example, let’s assume we are building an interface to describe the components necessary to define a car.
  GetColor() and  GetSpeed() make perfect sense, they are methods confined to the scope of a car.  IsFiretruck() is an anti-pattern. We are forcing all cars to declare whether or not they are firetrucks. In order for this pattern to make any amount of sense, we would need a whole list of possible subtypes.  IsPickup(),  IsSedan(),  IsTank()… where does it end??
 Instead, the developer should have relied on the native functionality of  type assertion to derive the underlying type when given an instance of the  car interface. Or, if a sub-interface is needed, it can be defined as:
  Which inherits the required methods from  car and adds one additional required method to make the car a  firetruck.
  Interfaces don’t have constructors or deconstructors that require that data is created or destroyed.
 Interfaces aren’t hierarchical by nature, though there is syntactic sugar to create interfaces that happen to be supersets of other interfaces.
 Interfaces define function signatures, but not underlying behavior. Making an interface often won’t  DRY up your code in regards to struct methods. For example, if five types satisfy the error interface, they all need their own version of the  Error() function.
title: 在Go中编写干净界面的最佳实践
title_en: Best Practices for Writing Clean Interfaces in Go

note:
Antoine Chambert-Loir加入数学界最古老的秘密社团之一始于一通电话。他说：“他们告诉我，布尔贝基想让我来看看我是否愿意和他们合作。”
钱伯特-卢伊尔接受了，在2001年9月的一周里，他每天花七个小时大声朗读数学课文，并与这个小组的成员讨论，他们的身份不为世界其他人所知。
他从未被正式邀请加入，但在最后一天，他被赋予了一项长期任务-完成该小组自1975年以来一直在研究的一份手稿。当钱伯特-卢伊尔后来收到一份会议报告时，他看到自己被列为“纪念品”，这表明他是该组织的一员。从那以后，他帮助推动了一种近乎西西弗式的数学写作传统，这种传统早在第二次世界大战之前就开始了。
该组织被称为“尼古拉斯·布尔贝基”，通常被称为“布尔巴基”。这个名字是从19世纪一位现实生活中的法国将军那里借来的一个集体笔名，他从未与数学有任何关系。目前还不清楚他们为什么选择这个名字，尽管这个名字可能起源于创始数学家在巴黎师范学院(ENS)读本科时的一个恶作剧。
巴黎大学(University Of Paris)数学家钱伯特-卢伊尔(Chambert-Loir)说，“在一年级的时候，有一些恶作剧的习俗，其中一个恶作剧就是假装某个布尔贝基将军会来参观学校，或许还会做一场完全晦涩难懂的数学演讲。”卢伊尔是该组织的发言人，也是该组织唯一公开身份的成员。
Bourbaki成立于1934年，是由一小部分最近的校友发起的。他们中的许多人都是同时代最优秀的数学家之一。但当他们调查他们的领域时，他们发现了一个问题。这个问题的确切性质也是神话的主题。
一言以蔽之，Bourbaki是对第一次世界大战中失去一代数学家的回应，在那之后，该组织的创始人希望找到一种方法来保存留在欧洲的数学知识。
雷恩大学(University Of Rennes)的塞巴斯蒂安·古泽尔(Sébastien Gouëzel)表示：“有一种说法是，年轻的法国数学家在(第一次)世界大战期间没有被视为政府的优先事项，许多人被派往战场，在那里阵亡。”古泽尔没有公开加入该组织，但像许多数学家一样，他熟悉该组织的活动。
在一种更平淡但可能也更有可能的渲染中，最初的Bourbaki成员只是对该领域的教科书感到不满，想要创造出更好的教科书。钱伯特-卢伊尔说：“我认为，一开始这只是一个非常具体的问题。”
不管他们的动机是什么，Bourbaki的创始人都开始写作。然而，他们没有编写教科书，而是创造了一些完全新颖的东西：没有参考任何外部来源的解释高等数学的独立书籍。
布尔贝基的第一本书是关于微分几何的，这反映了该团体一些早期成员的品味，比如亨利·卡尔坦(Henri Cartan)和安德烈·韦尔(AndréWeil)。但这个项目很快就扩大了，因为很难在不涉及其他许多概念的情况下解释一个数学概念。
古泽尔说：“他们意识到，如果他们想要干净利落地做这件事，他们需要(其他领域的)想法，而Bourbaki正在成长为一个巨大的人物。”
《布尔巴基》最显著的特点是写作风格：严谨、正式，只剩下逻辑上的细枝末节。这些书从头到尾清楚地阐述了数学定理，没有跳过任何步骤--在数学家中展示了一种不同寻常的彻底性。
古泽尔说：“在布尔巴基，基本上没有差距。”“它们非常精确。”
但这种精确度是有代价的：布尔巴基的书可能很难读懂。他们没有提供一个上下文叙述来解释概念的来源，而是让想法自己说话。
钱伯特-卢伊尔说：“从本质上讲，你不会对自己做了什么或为什么要这么做发表任何评论。”“你只需陈述事实并加以证明，仅此而已。”
波尔巴基将其独特的写作风格与独特的写作过程结合在一起。一旦一名成员提交了草稿，小组成员就会亲自聚集在一起，大声朗读，并建议修改笔记。他们重复这些步骤，直到一致同意文本可以出版。这是一个漫长的过程，可能需要十年或更长时间才能完成。
note_en:
Antoine Chambert-Loir’s initiation into one of math’s oldest secret societies began with a phone call. “They told me Bourbaki would like me to come and see if I’d work with them,” he said.
 Chambert-Loir accepted, and for a week in September 2001 he spent seven hours a day reading math texts out loud and discussing them with the members of the group, whose identities are unknown to the rest of the world.
 He was never officially asked to join, but on the last day he was given a long-term task — to finish a manuscript the group had been working on since 1975. When Chambert-Loir later received a report on the meeting he saw that he was listed as a “membrifié,” indicating he was part of the group. Ever since, he’s helped advance an almost Sisyphean tradition of math writing that predates World War II.
 The group is known as “Nicolas Bourbaki” and is usually referred to as just Bourbaki. The name is a collective pseudonym borrowed from a real-life 19th-century French general who never had anything to do with mathematics. It’s unclear why they chose the name, though it may have originated in a prank played by the founding mathematicians as undergraduates at the École Normale Supérieure (ENS) in Paris.
  “There was some custom to play pranks on first-years, and one of those pranks was to pretend that some General Bourbaki would arrive and visit the school and maybe give a totally obscure talk about mathematics,” said Chambert-Loir, a mathematician at the University of Paris who has acted as a spokesperson for the group and is its one publicly identified member.
 Bourbaki began in 1934, the initiative of a small number of recent ENS alumni. Many of them were among the best mathematicians of their generation. But as they surveyed their field, they saw a problem. The exact nature of that problem is also the subject of myth.
 In one telling, Bourbaki was a response to the loss of a generation of mathematicians to World War I, after which the group’s founders wanted to find a way to preserve what math knowledge remained in Europe.
 “There is a story that young French mathematicians were not seen as a government priority during [the] First World War and many were sent to war and died there,” said  Sébastien Gouëzel of the University of Rennes, who is not publicly identified with the group but, like many mathematicians, is familiar with its activities.
 In a more prosaic but probably also more likely rendering, the original Bourbaki members were simply dissatisfied with the field’s textbooks and wanted to create better ones. “I think at the beginning it was just for that very concrete matter,” Chambert-Loir said.
  Whatever their motivation, the founders of Bourbaki began to write. Yet instead of writing textbooks, they ended up creating something completely novel: free-standing books that explained advanced mathematics without reference to any outside sources.
 The first Bourbaki text was meant to be about differential geometry, which reflected the tastes of some of the group’s early members, luminaries like Henri Cartan and André Weil. But the project quickly expanded, since it’s hard to explain one mathematical idea without involving many others.
 “They realized that if they wanted to do this cleanly, they needed [ideas from other areas], and Bourbaki grew and grew into something huge,” Gouëzel said.
 The most distinctive feature of Bourbaki was the writing style: rigorous, formal and stripped to the logical studs. The books spelled out mathematical theorems from the ground up without skipping any steps — exhibiting an unusual degree of thoroughness among mathematicians.
 “In Bourbaki, essentially, there are no gaps,” Gouëzel said. “They are super precise.”
 But that precision comes at a cost: Bourbaki books can be hard to read. They don’t offer a contextualizing narrative that explains where concepts come from, instead letting the ideas speak for themselves.
 “Essentially, you give no comment about what you do or why you do it,” Chambert-Loir said. “You state stuff and prove it, and that’s it.”
 Bourbaki joined its distinctive writing style to a distinctive writing process. Once a member produces a draft, the group gathers in person, reads it aloud and suggests notes for revision. They repeat these steps until there is unanimous agreement that the text is ready for publication. It’s a long process that can take a decade or more to complete.
title: 秘密数学协会内部，简称尼古拉斯·布尔贝基(Nicolas Bourbaki)
title_en: Inside The Secret Math Society Known Simply as Nicolas Bourbaki

note:
华盛顿：陆军希望下一次战争的第一个受害者是机器人，而不是人类。但是，再多的高科技也不会允许一场不流血的胜利，本宁堡军种机动中心的新任指挥官警告说，该中心负责坦克和步兵训练。因此，少将帕特里克·多纳霍(Patrick Donahoe)和他的幕僚们没有设计出某种未来主义的全新力量，而是复兴了经受过战斗考验的冷战概念-比如坦克步兵团队合作和强大的师级编队-并用大量的无人驾驶系统对其进行了更新。
在阿富汗和伊拉克的游击战中，陆军加强了其旅的力量，基本上独立行动，师和兵团等更高梯队发挥辅助作用。多纳霍说，对于未来的大规模战争，陆军希望加强该师的力量，恢复本世纪头十年淘汰的旅级火炮和侦察(师骑兵)部队。这种远程火力部队和侦察部队(空中和地面)的结合将使师指挥官能够在远大于其下属旅所能覆盖的距离内作战和机动。
作为补偿，旅战斗队可能会交出他们自冷战以来积累的一些专业资产。多纳霍在NDIA武器、机器人和弹药(ARM)会议上表示：“BCT可能会更小，而且该部门将不得不为不同的任务进行扩充。”“但在技术上，它将更多地采用自主系统。”
多纳霍说，军方对最近的冲突--俄罗斯对乌克兰，亚美尼亚对阿塞拜疆--的研究表明，在一支以冷战为主的部队中加入少量21世纪的技术可以产生戏剧性的影响。多么?。俄罗斯采用的一种具有毁灭性影响的方法是使用无人机为火箭发射器寻找目标。同样，尽管美国陆军正在开发一系列新型导弹、装甲车和飞机，但大多数部队在未来几年都将使用里根时代的硬件。从本质上说，多纳霍希望将这些现有的武器组织成新的编队，并增加无人机和地面机器人来侦察前方。
多纳霍的做法与陆军中长期存在的科技狂热不一致。尤其是最近几个月，我注意到一些将军以各种形式背诵着2009年取消的未来作战系统计划的口头禅：技术将让美军“先看、先理解、先行动”的理念。多纳霍在周四的讲话中对这种技术乐观主义提出了异议。
多纳霍警告说：“我们从过去20年的经验中知道，技术飞跃是伟大的，但它们不能给你这种无所不知的世界观。”“这可以追溯到20世纪90年代末、21世纪初，当时我们在美国军界为这场‘军事革命’鼓点，(预测)我们将知道一切，我们将拥有这样的信息主导地位，我们将能够先见之明，行动先行(Action First…)。.(今天)，你会听到一些相同的观点。“。
唐纳德·桑多(Donald Sando)是多纳霍的文职副手，也是本宁堡(Fort Benning)多年来的知识分子中流砥柱。他说：“要警惕军事上的革命。”“我们不能无休止地指望技术会让战争变得更容易、更不残酷或成本更低，[因为]现实情况是，它不会；它会改变它。”在很多情况下，这会让事情变得更加困难。“。
多纳霍说：“重要的是，我们要尽最大努力利用技术。”“但我们永远不应该忘记，是地面上的士兵，是近距离战斗中的坦克乘员，是与敌人…逼近的步兵战车。坦克步兵队仍将是我们在近距离战斗中将意志强加给敌人的主要方式。“
多纳霍说，“这并不意味着我们要[派遣]这些力量进入绞肉机”，而不是先尝试书中的每一个高科技技巧。
桑多说，关于直接交火的历史数据“表明，我们的敌人通常80%的时间都是先开枪的。”“我们不喜欢这样的赔率，[所以]我们希望尽可能避免势均力敌的战斗。”如果我们不能避免，我们想在对我们有利的条件下进入。“。
但怎么做呢？现行的陆军教条规定“与最小的元素接触”。用外行的话说，如果你一定要碰上敌人而遭到枪击(正式的说法是“交战”)，那就尽可能用最小的先头部队去做，让主体有时间准备和机动，而不被压制。多纳霍说，未来的目标将是与无人驾驶飞行器进行首次接触。
冷战学说设想在所谓的前线部队(FOLOT)上与敌人交战。根据会议上的一份简报，在新的概念中，无人机系统前线(FLFA)将在无人区飞行进入敌方控制的领土，然后是地面上的机器人前线(FLOR)，然后是(人类)部队的前线。无人驾驶系统将赶走敌人，偶然遇到交战和伏击，接受和接受第一次打击，并为跟随在他们后面的人类部队绘制敌方位置。
当然，军队今天不能这么做。为了在现实中执行这一概念，他们需要更多的无人驾驶系统，所以他们将建造这些系统。
为了在敌人射杀人类士兵之前看清敌人，陆军希望向从远程飞机到掌上微型无人机等各个梯队的部队部署无人侦察机：
师长们已经有陆军版的捕食者，MQ-1C灰鹰；有一个长期计划，用新的先进无人机系统(AUAS)取代它，但到目前为止，这只是纯幻灯片。
各旅目前凑合着使用RQ-7B影子飞机，它需要一条跑道和大量的支援设备，其发动机噪音经常警告敌人躲避它的接近。未来战术无人机(FTUAS)计划目前正在对四种可能的替代品进行实地测试，所有这些替代品都设计为在没有跑道的情况下运行，支持最少，噪音要小得多。
现在各营都没有自己的无人侦察机，所以陆军正在制定被称为远程侦察(LRR)的小型无人机的正式要求。
公司将继续使用重达4磅的RQ-11B乌鸦，士兵们通过捡起它并将其扔向空中来发射它。
各排将获得一架近程侦察(SRR)微型无人机。一位简报人士表示，投产的决定尚未做出，如果按计划进行，第一批SRR将于2021年交付运营单位。
小队已经收到了士兵携带的传感器(SBS)包裹，其中包括两架黑蜂微型无人机--小到足以降落在你的手掌上--外加一个控制单元和充电站。
地面机器人落后于空中系统。实际上，给电脑编程，让它在空中飞行比绕过岩石、树木和沟渠要容易得多。但陆军正在开发新的无人地面车辆，用于侦察、战斗和补给：
机器人战斗车(RCV)重型基本上是一种无人驾驶的轻型坦克，重量在20到30吨之间。第一批试验性的无人驾驶汽车设计将于2023年进行现场测试。
德事隆在其Ripsaw微型坦克的基础上，正在建造一种10吨重的RCV-Medium，而Qinetiq正在建造7吨的RCV-Light。(这些合同只涵盖2022年试验的8辆车，而不是全面生产)。这些较小的机器人战斗车是多用途履带式机器，可以携带补给、传感器、干扰器或远程控制武器系统。
下一个尺寸是小型多功能设备运输车(S-MET)，这是一种轮式车辆，很像军用高尔夫球车。通用动力公司赢得了生产合同，陆军计划到2024年购买624架S-Met。S-MET的主要任务是跟随携带多达1000磅补给的步兵前进，但它也可以安装传感器，甚至是武器来侦察前方。
陆军还将把有人驾驶的补给卡车改装成无人驾驶的跟随者车辆，部署一种名为M160 Flail的履带式扫雷机器人，并对其遥控拆弹队式的机器人进行现代化改造。
一种新的获得专利的微波射频集成电路可以在行业领先的频率下运行，为软件定义的无线电开发高级应用。
新的太空作战负责人(CSO)规划指南建议：“虽然我们将在和平时期扩大和捍卫美国的竞争优势，但衡量我们准备能力的最终标准是，一旦战争开始或延伸到太空，我们是否有能力取胜。”

note_en:
WASHINGTON: The Army wants  the first casualty of the next war to be a robot, not a human being. But no amount of high technology will allow a bloodless victory, warned the  new commander of the service’s  Maneuver Center at Fort Benning, which runs tank and infantry training. So instead of devising some futuristic all-new force,  Maj. Gen. Patrick Donahoe and his staff are reviving battle-tested Cold War concepts – like tank-infantry teamwork and robust division-level formations  – and updating them with  a large dash of unmanned systems.
  In guerrilla warfare in Afghanistan and Iraq, the Army beefed up its brigades to operate largely independently, with higher echelons such as divisions and corps in a supporting role. For future large-scale wars, Donahoe said, the Army wants to strengthen the division, restoring the brigade-strength artillery and reconnaissance (“divisional cavalry”) elements eliminated in the 2000s. This combination of long range firepower  and scout forces — both air and ground — will allow division commanders to fight and maneuver over distances much larger than what their subordinate brigades can cover.
 To compensate, Brigade Combat Teams may yield some of the specialized assets they’ve accumulated since the Cold War. “The BCT will probably be smaller [and] will have to get augmented for different tasks by the division,” Donahoe told  the NDIA Armaments, Robotics, & Munitions (ARM) conference. “But it will be more technically technologically enabled [with] autonomous systems.”
  Army studies of recent conflicts – Russia vs. Ukraine, Armenia vs. Azerbaijan – shows you can have a dramatic impact by adding a small infusion of 21 st century tech to a largely Cold War force, Donahoe said. How? One approach the Russians have employed to devastating effect is to use drones to spot targets for rocket launchers. Likewise, while the US Army is developing a host of new missiles, armored vehicles, and aircraft, most units will be using Reagan-era hardware for years to come. In essence, Donahoe wants to organize these existing weapons in new formations and add drones and ground robots to scout ahead.
    Donahoe’s approach is at odds with a longstanding strain of technophilia in the Army. Particularly in recent months, I’ve noticed some generals reciting, in various forms, the mantra of  the Future Combat Systems program cancelled in 2009: the idea that technology would let US forces “see first, understand first, act first.” Donahoe took issue with that techno-optimism in his remarks on Thursday.
 “We know from our experience of the last 20 years that technological leaps are great, but they can’t give you this omniscient view of the world,” Donahoe warned. “This goes back to [the] late 1990s, early 2000s, when we had this drumbeat in United States military circles of this ‘ Revolution in Military Affairs,’ [predicting] we will know everything, we will have such information dominance, we will be able to see first, act first….[Today], you hear some of the same arguments being made.”
    “Be cautious of revolutions in military affairs,” said  Donald Sando, Donahoe’s civilian deputy and an intellectual mainstay at Fort Benning for many years. “We can’t hope endlessly that technology will make warfare easier or less brutal or less costly, [because] The reality is, it doesn’t; it  changes it. It makes it harder in many cases.”
  “It is important that we use technology to the absolute best we can,” said Donahoe. “But we should never forget that it’s the soldier on the ground, it’s the tank crew in close combat, it’s that infantry fighting vehicle closing with the enemy… The tank-infantry team will remain the primary way we impose our will on the enemy in close combat.”
 “That doesn’t mean we’re going to [send] those forces into a meatgrinder” without first trying every high-tech trick in the book, Donahoe said.
 Historical data on direct-fire engagements “shows that our enemies generally shoot first 80 percent of the time,” Sando said. “We don’t like those odds, [so] we want to avoid the close fight if we can. If we can’t avoid it, we want to enter it under conditions that are favorable to us.”
 But how? Current Army doctrine prescribes “making contact with the smallest element.” In layman’s terms, if you  must stumble upon the enemy and get shot at (the formal term for this is a, “meeting engagement”), then do it with the smallest vanguard possible, giving the main body time to prepare and maneuver without being pinned down. In the future, Donahoe said, the goal will be to make first contact with an  unmanned element.
 Cold War doctrine envisioned engaging the enemy along what’s called the  Forward Line Of Troops, or FLOT. In the new concept, according to a briefing at the conference, a  Forward Line Of Unmanned Aerial Systems (FLUA) will fly ahead through no-man’s-land into enemy-held territory, followed by a  Forward Line Of Robots (FLOR) on the ground, followed in turn by the Forward Line Of (Human) Troops. The unmanned systems will flush out the enemy, stumble into meeting engagements and ambushes, take and receive the first hits, and map the enemy position for the human troops coming along behind them.
 Of course, the Army can’t do this today. To execute the concept in reality, they need a lot more unmanned systems, so they’re going to build them.
   To see the enemy before they shoot a human soldier, the Army wants to issue reconnaissance drones to units at every echelon, from long-range aircraft to  palm-top micro-drones:
 Division commanders already have the Army version of the Predator, the  MQ-1C Grey Eagle; there’s a long-term plan to replace this with a new  Advanced Unmanned Aerial System (AUAS) but this is pure PowerPoint so far.
 Brigades currently make do with the  RQ-7B Shadow, which requires a runway and  extensive support equipment, and whose engine noise often warns enemies to hide from its approach. The  Future Tactical UAS (FTUAS) program is now  field-testing four possible replacements, all of them designed to operate without a runway, with minimal support, and with much less noise.
 Battalions don’t have their own scout drones today, so the Army is developing formal requirements for small UAS known as  Long-Range Recon (LRR).
 Companies will continue to use the  four-pound  RQ-11B Raven, which soldiers launch by picking it up and throwing it into the air.
 Platoons will get a  Short-Range Recon (SRR) mini-drone. A decision to start production is pending, a briefer said, and if that goes ahead as planned, the first SRRs will go to operational units in 2021.
 Squads are already receiving the  Soldier-Borne Sensor (SBS) package, which consist of two  Black Hornet micro-drones – small enough to land on the palm of your hand – plus a control unit and charging station.
 Ground robots lag behind the aerial systems. It’s actually easier to program a computer to fly through empty air than to  maneuver around rocks, trees, and ditches. But the Army is developing new Unmanned Ground Vehicles for reconnaissance, combat, and resupply:
 The  Robotic Combat Vehicle (RCV) Heavy is basically an unmanned light tank, in the 20 to 30-ton range. The first experimental RCV designs will be field-tested in 2023.
 Textron is building an experimental 10-ton  RCV-Medium, based on its Ripsaw mini-tank, while Qinetiq is building the seven-ton  RCV-Light. (The contracts cover just eight vehicles for experiments in 2022, not full-scale production). These  smaller Robotic Combat Vehicles are multi-purpose tracked machines that can carry supplies, sensors,  jammers, or  remote-controlled weapon systems.
 The next size down is the  Small Multipurpose Equipment Transport (S-MET), a wheeled vehicle much like a militarized golfcart.  General Dynamics won the production contract, and the Army  plans to acquire 624 S-METs through 2024. The S-MET’s primary mission is to trundle along behind foot troops carrying up to  1,000 pounds of supplies, but it could also be fitted with sensors and even weapons to scout ahead.
 The Army is also converting manned supply trucks into self-driving  Leader-Follower vehicles, fielding a tracked mine-clearing robot called the  M160 Flail, and modernizing its fleet of remote-controlled bomb-squad-style robots.
  A new, patented Microwave radio-frequency integrated circuit can operate at industry-leading frequencies to develop advanced applications for software-defined radios.
 Recommended  “While we will extend and defend America’s competitive advantage in peacetime, the ultimate measure of our readiness is the ability to prevail should war initiate in, or extend to space,” the new Chief of Space Operations (CSO) Planning Guidance says.

note:
亚美尼亚、阿塞拜疆和俄罗斯签署协议，结束围绕有争议的纳戈尔诺-卡拉巴赫飞地的军事冲突。
亚美尼亚总理尼科尔·帕辛扬(Nikol Pashinyan)称，这笔交易对我和我们的人民来说都是令人难以置信的痛苦。
该地区在国际上被公认为阿塞拜疆人，但自1994年以来一直由亚美尼亚人统治。
自9月再次爆发战斗以来，双方促成了多项停火协议，但都以失败告终。
根据新协议，阿塞拜疆将保留在冲突期间占领的纳戈尔诺-卡拉巴赫地区。亚美尼亚还同意在未来几周内从其他几个邻近地区撤军。
在一次电视在线讲话中，俄罗斯总统弗拉基米尔·普京(Vladimir Putin)表示，1960名俄罗斯维和人员将被部署到前线巡逻。
据阿塞拜疆总统伊尔哈姆·阿利耶夫(Ilham Aliyev)说，土耳其也将参加维和进程。阿利耶夫与普京总统一起发表了讲话。
普京总统表示，该协议将包括交换战俘，所有经济和运输联系都将畅通无阻。
阿利耶夫总统说，这项协议具有历史意义，相当于亚美尼亚投降。
亚美尼亚总理帕辛扬说，他的决定是基于对战斗形势的深入分析，并与该领域最优秀的专家进行了讨论。
帕辛扬表示：这不是胜利，但除非你认为自己被打败了，否则就不算失败。
纳戈尔诺-卡拉巴赫的亚美尼亚领导人阿拉伊克·哈鲁图尼扬(Arayi K Harutyunyan)表示，他同意尽快结束战争。
据当地媒体报道，在亚美尼亚首都埃里温，一大群人聚集在一起抗议这项协议。
亚美尼亚人正在稳步失去领土，上周末，阿塞拜疆军队占领了该地区第二大城镇舒沙，亚美尼亚人称之为舒什。
阿塞拜疆还承认在亚美尼亚上空错误击落了一架俄罗斯军用直升机，造成两名机组人员死亡，第三人受伤。
目前还不清楚到底有多少人死亡。双方都否认以平民为目标，但指责对方这样做。
纳戈尔诺-卡拉巴赫当局表示，近1200名国防人员在战斗中丧生，平民也被打死或受伤。
阿塞拜疆尚未公布其军事伤亡数字，但表示已有80多名平民在战斗中丧生，其中包括上个月对巴尔达镇发动的导弹袭击造成的21人死亡。
俄罗斯总统弗拉基米尔·普京(Vladimir Putin)上个月表示，近5000人在战斗中丧生。
俄罗斯在亚美尼亚有一个军事基地，两国是莫斯科领导的集体安全条约组织的成员。
该条约设想，如果亚美尼亚受到攻击，俄罗斯将提供军事支持--但不包括被亚美尼亚军队占领的纳戈尔诺-卡拉巴赫(Nagorno-Karabakh)或周边其他阿塞拜疆地区。
与此同时，莫斯科也与阿塞拜疆有着密切的联系，阿塞拜疆正得到北约成员国土耳其的公开支持。俄罗斯一直在向亚美尼亚和阿塞拜疆出售武器。
note_en:
Armenia, Azerbaijan and Russia have signed an agreement to end military conflict over the disputed enclave of Nagorno-Karabakh.
 Armenian Prime Minister Nikol Pashinyan called the deal "incredibly painful both for me and both for our people".
  The region is internationally recognised as Azerbaijani, but has been run by ethnic Armenians since 1994.
  A number of ceasefire agreements have been brokered since fighting broke out again in September, but all of them have failed.
   Under the new deal, Azerbaijan will hold onto areas of Nagorno-Karabakh that it has taken during the conflict. Armenia has also agreed to withdraw from several other adjacent areas over the next few weeks.
  During a televised online address, Russia's President Vladimir Putin said that 1,960 Russian peacekeepers would be deployed to patrol frontlines.
 Turkish will also take part in the peacekeeping process, according to Azeri President Ilham Aliyev, who joined President Putin during the address.
  President Putin said the agreement would include an exchange of war prisoners, with "all economical and transport contacts to be un-blocked."
  President Aliyev said the agreement was of "historic importance," and amounted to a "capitulation" by Armenia.
  Armenian PM Pashinyan said that his decision was based on "deep analyses of the combat situation and in discussion with best experts of the field".
 "This is not a victory but there is not defeat until you consider yourself defeated," Mr Pashinyan said.
  The Armenian leader in Nagorno-Karabakh, Arayik Harutyunyan, said he gave his agreement "to end the war as soon as possible".
 In the Armenian capital Yerevan, a large crowd has gathered to protest against the agreement, according to local media.
  The Armenians have steadily lost territory and over the weekend Azeri forces took over the region's second largest town Shusha, known as Shushi in Armenian.
 Azerbaijan has also admitted to mistakenly shooting down a Russian military helicopter over Armenia, killing two crew members and injuring a third.
  It is unclear exactly how many have died. Both sides deny targeting civilians but accuse the other of doing so.
 Nagorno-Karabakh's authorities say nearly 1,200 of its defence forces have died in the fighting, and civilians have also been killed or injured.
 Azerbaijan has not released its military casualty figures but has said more than 80 civilians have been killed in the fighting - including 21 in a missile strike on the town of Barda last month.
 Russian President Vladimir Putin said last month that almost 5,000 people had been killed in the fighting.
   Russia has a military base in Armenia, and the two countries are members of the Moscow-led Collective Security Treaty Organization.
 The treaty envisages Russia's military support if Armenia is attacked - but it does not include Nagorno-Karabakh or the other Azerbaijani regions around it seized by Armenian forces.
 At the same time, Moscow also has strong ties to Azerbaijan, which is being openly backed by Turkey, a Nato member. Russia has been selling weapons to both Armenia and Azerbaijan.

note:
2014年创立Balanx的张说：“我们目前并不是真正针对中国消费者。”“这套西装是为西方更精明的消费者设计的。”
硬件制造商的前景看起来一直很光明，直到两年前，特朗普政府开始对中国设置贸易壁垒。从北京对香港的政策到冠状病毒大流行，两国关系在一系列引爆点事件中不断恶化。
根据TechCrunch与七家中国硬件初创企业的对话，中国企业家预计两国关系不会很快回暖，但许多人确实相信，新办公室将做出“不那么反复无常”和“更理性”的政策决定。中国的科技企业，无论大小，都在迅速适应美中竞争的新时代，继续吸引海外客户。
张只是众多希望将最先进的中国硬件带到世界上的企业家之一。这一代创始人不再兜售廉价的电子模仿品，这是旧的“中国制造”制度的形象。几十年的知识转移、产品开发、制造、出口实践和政策支持，使中国成为生产既尖端又普遍负担得起的新技术的强国。
安克的电力银行、Roborock的吸尘器和华米的健身跟踪器只是几个在海外市场获得忠实追随者的产品，更不用说华为、小米、OPPO和大疆等全球家喻户晓的品牌了。
消费者情绪也在发生变化。在过去的10到15年里，欧洲人对“中国制造”的质量和创新的认知有了“显著的提高”，小米支持的Dreame负责市场营销的Frank Wang说，该公司生产高端家用电器，包括戴森吹风机和吸尘器的更便宜的替代品。
新玩家渴望复制前辈的成功。他们在CES等国际交易会上寻求媒体关注和零售合作伙伴，自学Facebook和谷歌的宣传活动，并在众筹平台上讨好电子产品爱好者。从GGV Capital到小米等投资者争相支持活力四射的初创企业，这些初创企业已经向全球发运了数百万部手机。
对于总部位于深圳的硬件公司电子零部件供应商Donny Zhang来说，贸易战一打响，业务就一直在萎缩。“我的客户首当其冲，因为采购成本增加了，”他在谈到那些直接或间接与美国公司打交道的客户时说。
虽然许多以出口为导向的硬件企业厌恶盈利能力下降，但一些企业学会了适应并寻找一线希望。这出人意料地给中国的工厂老板带来了新的方向。Indiegogo是世界上最大的众筹平台之一，它亲眼目睹了这些变化。
Indiegogo全球战略总经理陆莉告诉TechCrunch：“一旦关税提高，制造商的利润率就所剩无几了，因为中间商已经吃掉了他们的大部分利润。”
“一个好的解决方案是工厂跳过中间商，直接销售给有自主品牌的消费者。一旦品牌建设的目标明确，他们往往会来找我们，因为他们需要营销帮助，作为将自己打造为全球消费品牌的第一步。“。
这一趋势被称为“直接面向消费者”(D2C)，也符合中国鼓励制造业升级和自主创新以参与全球竞争的国家计划，这一倡议在2015年左右开始形成。这一发展自然使中国Indiegogo成为过去两年增长最快的地区：据李说，在2020年前三个季度，来自中国的企业同比增长了50%。
拥有一个有吸引力的产品和品牌只是前提。不断变化的贸易政策和地缘政治迫使许多中国企业认真本地化，无论这意味着建立一个外国实体还是建立一个本地团队。
对于为世界各地的设备制造商提供物联网解决方案的途亚来说，自2015年以来，该公司一直在运营一家美国实体，雇佣了当地的销售和技术支持人员，因此贸易战的影响一直很小。然而，TikTok的大部分研发仍然掌握在印度和中国的工程师手中，后者可能是一个潜在的争议点，TikTok最近在美国的反弹就表明了这一点。
他说：“关键是合规。我们有一个专门的安全专家团队来解决合规性问题。例如，我们是首批在欧洲获得GDPR认证的公司之一，“该公司的首席营销部伊娃·纳(Eva Na)说。
不过，该公司做好准备是出于实际需要。它的许多客户是要求供应商严格遵守法律规定的西方大公司，所以图亚很早就开始收集所需的证书。途亚今天连接了20万个SKU，足迹遍及190多个海外国家，占其业务的60%以上。
资金雄厚的途亚可能有足够的财务和运营能力维持一个海外团队；但对于规模较小的初创企业来说，本地化可能是一个昂贵而乏味的学习曲线。许多人选择在香港设立一家实体，以利用香港作为全球金融中心的地位，规避对中国内地的贸易限制，这是香港的一个优势，在北京实施国家安全法后，香港开始崩溃。
智能运动服制造商Balanx与许多面向出口的硬件同行一样，也在香港设有分支机构。为了应对新的全球逆风，该公司在内华达州注册了一家虚拟公司，但很快意识到，除非在美国有实地业务，否则这个实体几乎没有什么用处。
“如果我想开立账户，很多当地银行都会索要水电费之类的东西，而我们现在还没有。”我们意识到我们必须拥有一支本土团队，“这位创始人断言。
张乐观地认为，尽管美国实施了制裁，但像他自己这样的小公司仍将不受关注。“只要避免与政府有任何联系，”他说。
事实上，一些更“良性”和小众的产品正继续在全球推广中蓬勃发展。小米支持的初创公司PopuMusic就是其中之一，该公司生产四弦琴和吉他等智能乐器，供初学者学习。“我们没有受到贸易战的影响。我们所处的行业既不具威胁性，也不具侵略性。“PopuMusic创始人张伯翰表示。PopuMusic将美国视为其最大的海外市场之一。
随着冠状病毒席卷全球，中国品牌也看到了自己的优势，并将数百万人限制在国内。在一个网购无处不在的国家，Balanx、Dreame和PopuMusic等硬件制造商早就学会了掌握电子商务和物流。
“欧洲和美国的消费者越来越习惯于电子商务，有点像五到八年前的中国，”梦想网的王说。
PopuMusic没有重新考虑美国市场，而是通过Indiegogo活动推出了一款新的联网吉他，从而进一步向前迈进。这位创始人表示，全球扩张是这家初创公司愿景的核心。他说：“我们从第一天起就是全球化的。我们甚至在想出中文名字之前就有了英文名字。“
Indiegogo的李表示，在赚大钱的过程中，硬件制造商可能不得不淡化他们的“中国制造”或“中国设计”品牌。这可以帮助他们在国际努力中避免不必要的地缘政治复杂和关注。但人们不禁要问，这一代新一代企业家是如何看待他们的民族自豪感的。他们如何应对北京下达的在全球市场推动中国创新的使命？这是中国企业家在未来几年的全球之旅中必须谨慎遵循的一条路线。
note_en:
“We are not really aiming at Chinese consumers at this point,” said Zhang, who started  Balanx in 2014. “The suit is for the more savvy consumers in the West.”
 Prospects for hardware makers were looking bright until two years ago when the Trump administration began setting trade barriers on China. Relations between the two countries have been  deteriorating over a series of flashpoint events, from  Beijing’s policy on Hong Kong to the  coronavirus pandemic.
  Chinese entrepreneurs don’t expect relationships between the countries to warm up anytime soon, but many do believe the new office will make “less erratic” and “more rational” policy decisions, according to conversations TechCrunch had with seven Chinese hardware startups. Chinese tech businesses, big or small, are adapting swiftly in the new era of U.S.-China competition as they continue to woo overseas customers.
  Zhang is just one of the many entrepreneurs looking to bring state-of-the-art Chinese hardware to the world. This generation of founders no longer hawk cheap electronic copycats, the image attached to the old “Made in China” regime. Decades of knowledge transfer, product development, manufacturing, export practice and policy support have made China a powerhouse for producing new technologies that are both edgy and still widely affordable.
  Anker’s power banks, Roborock’s vacuums and  Huami’s fitness trackers are just a few items that have gained loyal followings in several overseas markets, not to mention global household names like Huawei, Xiaomi, Oppo and DJI.
 Consumer sentiment is also changing. Europeans’ perception of “Made in China” quality and innovation has “improved significantly” over the last 10 to 15 years, said Frank Wang who oversees marketing at  Xiaomi  -backed Dreame which makes premium home appliances including  cheaper alternatives to Dyson hairdryers and vacuums.
 The new players are eager to replicate the success of their predecessors. They seek media attention and retail partners at international trade fairs like CES, teach themselves Facebook and Google campaigns, and court gadget lovers on crowdfunding platforms. Investors ranging from GGV Capital to Xiaomi rush to back scrappy startups that are already shipping millions of units around the globe.
 For Donny Zhang, a Shenzhen-based electronics parts supplier to hardware companies, businesses have been shrinking as soon as the trade war began. “My clients are taking the brunt because the costs of procurement have increased,” he said of those who directly or indirectly deal with American firms.
 While many export-led hardware businesses loathe decreasing profitability, some learn to adapt and look for a silver lining. That has unexpectedly spurred new directions for factory owners in China.  Indiegogo,   one of the world’s largest crowd-funding platforms, saw the changes first hand.
 “Once tariffs increase, there’s not much profit margin left for manufacturers because the middlemen already eat up the bulk of their profit,” Lu Li, general manager for Indiegogo’s global strategy, told TechCrunch.
 “A good solution is for factories to skip the middlemen and sell directly to consumers with their own brands. Once the goal of brand building is clear, they often come to us because they need marketing help as a first step to establish themselves as a global consumer brand.”
 The trend, dubbed “direct-to-consumers” or D2C, also plays into China’s national plan to encourage manufacturing upgrade and homegrown innovations to compete globally, an initiative that began to take shape around 2015. The development naturally makes China Indiegogo’s fastest-growing region in the last two years: in the first three quarters of 2020, businesses coming from China jumped 50% year-over-year, according to Li.
  Having an appealing product and brand is just the prerequisite. Ever-changing trade policies and geopolitics have forced many Chinese businesses to localize seriously, whether that means setting up a foreign entity or building a local team.
  For  Tuya, which provides IoT solutions to device makers around the world, the trade war’s effect has been “minimal” since it has operated a U.S. entity since 2015, which employs its local sales and technical support staff. Most of its research and development, however, still lies in the hands of its engineers in India and China, the latter of which can be a potential contention point, as shown by  TikTok’s recent backlash in the U.S.
 “The key is compliance. We have a dedicated team of security experts to work on compliance issues. For instance, we were one of the first to get GDPR certified in Europe,” said the company’s chief marketing office Eva Na.
 The company’s readiness is prompted by practical needs though. Many of its clients are large Western corporations that demand strict legal compliance in vendors, so Tuya began collecting the needed certificates early on. Connecting 200,000 SKUs today, Tuya’s footprint is found in over 190 overseas countries, which account for over 60% of its business.
 Well-funded Tuya may have the financial and operational capacity to sustain an overseas team; but for smaller startups, localization can be a costly and tedious learning curve. Many opted to set up a Hong Kong entity to tap the city’s status as a global financial hub and evade trade restrictions on China, an advantage of the territory that  began to crumble following Beijing’s implementation of the national security law.
 Balanx, the smart training suit maker, has a Hong Kong entity like many of its export-facing hardware peers. To cope with new global headwinds, it registered a virtual company in Nevada but quickly realized the entity is of little use unless it has an on-the-ground operation in the U.S.
 “Many local banks would ask for utility bills and etc. if I want to open an account, which we don’t have. We realized we must have a local team,” asserted the founder.
  Zhang is positive that small companies like his own will remain under the radar in spite of U.S. sanctions. “Just avoid having any government connection,” he said.
  Indeed, some of the more “benign” and niche products are continuing to thrive in their global push.  PopuMusic, a Xiaomi-backed startup making smart instruments like ukulele and guitar to teach beginners, is one. “We aren’t affected by the trade war. We are in a business that’s neither threatening nor aggressive,” said Zhang Bohan, founder of PopuMusic, which counts the U.S. as one of its biggest overseas markets.
 Chinese brands are also seeing their edge as the coronavirus sweeps across the globe and confines millions at home. Hardware makers like Balanx,  Dreame   and PopuMusic have long learned to master e-commerce and logistics in a country where online shopping is ubiquitous.
 “Consumers in Europe and the U.S. are growing more accustomed to e-commerce, a bit like those in China five to eight years ago,” said Wang of Dreame.
 Rather than rethinking the U.S., PopuMusic is forging further ahead by launching a new  connected guitar via an Indiegogo campaign. Global expansion is at the core of the startup’s vision, the founder said. “We are global from day one. We had an English name before even coming up with a Chinese one.”
 In the process of making big bucks, hardware makers may have to downplay their “Made in China” or “Designed in China” brand, said Li of Indiegogo. This could help them avoid unnecessary geopolitical complications and attention in their international push. But one has to wonder how this new generation of entrepreneurs is reckoning with their national pride. How do they deal with the mission passed down by Beijing to promote Chinese innovation in the global marketplace? It’s a line that Chinese entrepreneurs have to tread carefully in their global journey in the years to come.
title: 尽管面临全球逆风，中国硬件初创企业仍将挑战世界
title_en: Despite global headwinds, Chinese hardware startups remain to take on the world
